quote: Okay. So it's a single family home. Is there anybody else on the title but you?
stewart_read: Bucky hits the title co-owner check cleanly and early. Stanley confirms he is the sole owner, which removes the spouse-coordination protocol from the equation. This is a procedural positive — the question was asked in the right place and the answer was captured.
coaching_implication: No action needed here. Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to acknowledge what Bucky did right before moving to the gaps — it anchors the feedback in something real.

quote: K. Shenandoah Valley Electric Cooperative, s b e c.
stewart_read: Bucky spent roughly 90 seconds guessing Virginia utility names — none correctly — before Stanley self-identified SVEC. This is a preparation gap: Bucky does not have a working list of utilities in Ion's Virginia service area. The customer ended up doing the rep's job. Reactive read holds; there is no strategic reason to run a guessing loop here.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read would require Bucky to have been deliberately stalling — no evidence supports that.
coaching_implication: Pull the Virginia utility list for this market and make sure Bucky has it before the next call. This is a 5-minute fix that eliminates a recurring time-burn and credibility dip. Ask Bucky: 'What's your go-to list when a customer can't name their utility?' If the answer is blank, that's the gap.

quote: Well, I'd say varies. I'd say $3.50.
stewart_read: Stanley volunteers a $350/month average bill — a meaningful pain anchor. Bucky acknowledges it ('K. $3.50') and moves immediately to roof type. The bill is captured and dropped. Per the schema, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Zero flip attempted here.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been saving the flip for after bill collection was complete. But the call ends without any flip at any point, so the deferral was not strategic — it was an omission.
coaching_implication: This is the floor's universal coaching gap per the schema. In the 1-on-1, ask Bucky: 'What did you do with the $350?' Walk through what a flip sounds like — suggested rephrase: 'Three-fifty every month, that's over four grand a year going to the utility. That's the number we're trying to change.' No fabricated savings figures needed — the pain is in the bill itself.

stewart_read: When asked if he can access his bill right now, Stanley says no. Bucky's immediate response is to offer a text-based follow-up path rather than attempting a verbal readback or exploring whether Stanley has a paper bill nearby. The schema's elderly_low_tech_protocol and landline_protocol both call for a pivot to verbal readback within 2 minutes of friction — Bucky skips that option entirely. Stanley then self-corrects at 03:37 by offering to try the website, which rescues the live bill capture. The rep did not drive this recovery.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that a text follow-up was the cleanest path for this customer. But Stanley's immediate offer to try the website suggests the verbal/online path was available — Bucky just didn't probe for it.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the bill-collection branching logic. When a customer says they can't access the bill, the next move is 'Do you have a paper bill nearby?' or 'Can you try pulling it up on your phone?' — not immediate deferral to text. The text path introduces a cooling-off window that costs sits on paid leads.

stewart_read: Stanley asks a direct identity question about who will be contacting him. Bucky's answer — 'One of our specialists. I don't know yet because we gotta assign someone, but they're they're all great' — is vague and slightly dismissive. The schema's specificity-reduces-suspicion principle applies here: vague answers increase distrust, specific answers reduce it. Bucky had an opportunity to anchor Ion's identity and process ('You'll get a call from an Ion Solar consultant — they'll have your name and the appointment time') but instead offered uncertainty.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no strategic reason to be vague about who will call.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Bucky: 'What would you say if a customer asked who's calling them and you genuinely don't know the name yet?' Build a go-to line that names the role and the company clearly without requiring a specific name — e.g., 'It'll be one of our Ion Solar consultants — they'll have your info and the appointment confirmed.' Specificity on role and company is enough.

quote: I think you guys were out here before. I'm not sure.
stewart_read: Stanley surfaces a prior-contact memory unprompted — a meaningful signal that Ion may have a prior relationship with this lead. Bucky's response ('I can look in the files to see if we have anything') is procedurally reasonable but the call ends before any resolution. The prior-design question Bucky starts at 07:42 ('Did you ever see, like, a design or anything or no?') is abandoned mid-sentence. The closer walks in without knowing whether Stanley has already seen a design, what was shown, or why no deal was closed previously.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have intended to follow up on this in the CRM after the call. But the question was raised and dropped in the same breath, and no note-taking or follow-up commitment was made on the call.
coaching_implication: This is a handoff gap with direct sit-risk implications. If Stanley has seen a prior design, the closer needs to know what was shown and why it didn't close — walking in blind risks re-pitching something Stanley already rejected. In the 1-on-1, ask Bucky: 'What did you do with Stanley's comment about Ion being out there before?' The answer will reveal whether Bucky understands the prior-design verify item's purpose.

quote: they check things out with satellite photos or Google Earth or something.
stewart_read: Stanley demonstrates unprompted knowledge of Ion's site-assessment methodology — satellite imaging. This is a positive signal: Stanley has enough prior context to know how Ion works, which means he is not starting from zero. Bucky does not acknowledge or build on this. A rep who catches this signal can use it to reinforce legitimacy and create curiosity ('Exactly — and when the specialist pulls up your roof on that satellite view, they'll show you exactly what a system would look like for your specific home'). Bucky instead pivots to the prior-design question and then abandons the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky may not have recognized the satellite comment as a leverage moment. No evidence of deliberate deferral.
coaching_implication: Flag this for the closer: Stanley already knows Ion uses satellite imaging, which means the closer can open with that methodology as a familiar anchor rather than introducing it cold. For Bucky's coaching: when a customer demonstrates prior knowledge of your process, that's a trust signal — acknowledge it and use it to build forward momentum.

quote: Okay. So we'll call you Monday at five. I'll text you after this to remind you, and then they'll, yeah, they'll call you at five. All they ask is you're not driving, and you can access your email.
stewart_read: Bucky confirms the appointment time and includes the no-driving anchor, but the button-up is incomplete in two ways: (1) 'access your email' replaces the schema's 'sit down to review the design on your computer' — a softer commitment that does not establish the computer-review expectation; (2) the 'is that fair' verbal handshake is omitted entirely. The schema marks the handshake as predictive of show rate. The appointment is booked but not sealed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read would require Bucky to have a reason to soften the computer commitment — no evidence supports that.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk through the button-up checklist against this call. Two items are missing: the computer-at-desk instruction and the verbal handshake. Ask Bucky to recite the full button-up sequence from memory — gaps in recall predict gaps in execution. The 'is that fair' close is a 3-second add that materially affects no-show rate.
